speaker: interviewer
text: --96 In Sydney, Australia. Elizabeth, you were telling us about your stepmother. She wanted to bring her children to live in your father's house.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, after the war, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling me that the stepmother actually sent her daughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, after me, to the country.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Balaton?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, to Balaton.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because she was a Jewish--

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, she was Jewish. Because when she born, her mother was a Jewish, and her father was a Jewish. But she was in Budapest, and-- because his-- her father live in Budapest, not Ujpest. After they divorced, she-- he went.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did her father survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she-- he died before.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your--

speaker: interviewee
text: He died in the-- in the-- in the Budapest ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so your stepmother sent her daughter to be with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look. I-- I don't mind, because if she wanted to live the same way like I do, but she don't. She was a different type of person. She was too ladylike. That's what she think.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and she don't like the [LAUGHS] moo cows and the [LAUGHS] things.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went to get her in the railway station. And I went with the-- with the [NON-ENGLISH]-- with the-- with the moo cow was in the front, you know? And I went in the front with the-- stick, and I-- [LAUGHS] --it was-- Pushing.

speaker: interviewee
text: --no shoe, you know? And I went to see her. And I looking. And she looking. She don't think I am me. And I don't think she's her, you know? I said, what is that woman doing here? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I go to her. I said, what's your name? My God, you are a [NON-ENGLISH]!

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and that's how we meet. But did she travel on her own papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: She had false papers too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. She have to. Nobody can-- a Jewish person was not allowed to go in the train. And they have to wear a star. So, no, she--

speaker: interviewer
text: And she didn't look Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She don't. Of course not, she was 1/2 Jewish, so she don't look so Jewish. And she looks like-- exactly like her mother. [LAUGHS] So--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went back to Rakospalota with the priest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the-- the housekeeper-- next to the housekeeper. [LAUGHS] I was a second housekeeper-- and cooking, and cleaning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who else lived in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: 10 priests-- young priests.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there a church or a chapel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of-- of course, a church. He build a church. He built that church. That Dr. Roberts, he-- he build that church. That was a-- a railway workers' church, a railway-- a railway workers' town. All the people who working in a railway, they live in that town. And he build that church. He's-- he-- he-- he get the money. He-- he went to America. He speak English. And he a very, very clever man.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you lived with him-- cooking, cleaning?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. And-- and help him to the-- to the church. And one day was a wedding. Of course, I have to prepare the wedding. They bring the bride in, and you know, with the candles and the-- and it was my school friend. I was in school together. I think I dropped dead. But she was so excited, she don't recognize me. I give her the candles like that, you know, to hold it. But it was such a experience. I think I dropped dead. [LAUGHS] But--

speaker: interviewer
text: She didn't recognize you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she don't recognize me. Because--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you tell the priest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, after, after. Oh! And we-- in the nighttime, we make the book. And we give people a forged christening letters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which people? Who did you give it to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who-- any-- any-- anybody who come and ask, we give it to-- of the-- the priest give it to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish people came mainly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so they-- what did he do-- give them christening papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he charge for this?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he sympathetic towards the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, he always, all his life he-- he was sympathetic, because-- maybe that's why he was a friend of my father. But he said, it's actually, we all coming from there, you know. He was not a-- oh-- blinded Catholic. I-- I mean, you know, he-- he knows Jesus Christ was Jew. So?

speaker: interviewer
text: And at this stage, did you become friendly with the other priests as well in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course! I-- I clean for them, you know. And I cook for them. And we was very, very friendly. And we was-- have a-- a Jewish priest who was-- or Jewish boy, who become a priest. And his family live in Budapest. And he went to visit them. And he never come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he know you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he ever suspect?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, no, no-- nobody, nobody-- no-- nobody ever expected, because I don't looks like, and I don't-- don't act like, you know. Because I always was with-- with my friends, and-- and how I say? The-- my-- mine grandfather wasn't a Jew, so I never was a typical Jewish girl. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in the priests' house?

speaker: interviewee
text: After when-- when the Russians are come in.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The February, 1945. And I think in March time-- yes-- I-- I wanted to go home, because I wanted to fix our house. I was hoping my mother maybe come back. That time I don't know my mother not alive, you know? And I was hoping my brother will come home. He was only 22. I don't know he'd-- what's happening to him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't want they should have to go, like I have to go to be a housekeeper, and do that, and do that. I want-- I want to wait for them with the home the-- and a family, you know? And I was hoping they coming home, but-- [SIGHS] it's not.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- but I don't know that time. And I wanted to build up a-- a home for them. That was all my effort-- to put the things together. And how I said, we-- we take most of the furnishing to my father's place. And I wanted to have a home. So I find my cousin, one of my cousin coming home, and who is the-- or who was the richest in the-- in my town. And she come home with her husband and two little girl. And she find her fortune. They have a-- a furnishing factory. And she find the-- the factory, and was a-- a [NON-ENGLISH] Furnishing, which was a big effort at that time, and I think how many kilo of gold and things. She--

speaker: interviewer
text: She still had it all?

speaker: interviewee
text: She find it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- was under the ground.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the--

speaker: interviewee
text: And she knows where it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the garden, or in the-- in the building?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the factory-- in their factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the factory had been closed up?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, because of the Germans are working in the factory. And they used to have the beautifulest home in the world-- so-- in the-- in the city, in my town, and the big common-- the-- the big-- [NON-ENGLISH] over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Commander.

speaker: interviewee
text: --commander, he live in their home. So that she find everything-- the Persian carpets, the piano, and everything, because they live there. And they put the-- the gold in their factory, because they knows they working there. They will don't--

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH] .

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and they-- they don't think the stupid Jew will put it over there-- the-- the jewelry, you know? And he did. And she find it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she found it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So before you go on, when the Russians came, that was then the end of the war for you? It was already liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I feel thanks for them, you know, because if they not come, we are not surviving, you know? But--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the Russians behave when they came?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look. It's-- I always said, if you take-- keep the distance from them, they was all right. But if you started to friendly and not friendly, and you know-- it-- it-- it depends how you act. That-- that was my opinion. And I was a-- a younger, pretty girl. And they don't want me. They-- [LAUGHS] because I don't give them a chance to look at me that way. They always see me working, and you know, do that, and do that, and cleaning. So it was no hanky panky in my eyes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they-- they leave me alone. They-- they go to the other people who-- who want a hanky panky. They was-- they was nice to me. I can say that. So you continued working for the priest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, till-- till March, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I go to my cousin place. And I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go back to your own home first-- in Ujpest?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because somebody lived-- yeah, of course. She live in Ujpest. too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went to your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I-- I no. Because people lived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know people were living there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Had of course-- how the-- when we move out, they moved in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were the same people living there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to have a look at it at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in the first few weeks. No, I can't.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed with my cousin. She go back to her place. It's a beautiful home. And-- and I was her housekeeper. And I could-- the-- the children. She has two children that time. And I cooking, and I cleaning. And they-- they have to give one room to the Russian soldier. So I clean after them, and have-- them-- they-- they bring me some food, [LAUGHS] make business with them. I mean to-- to the children--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --have a food, because there was no food-- no food after the war. We go to the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: The communal kitchen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have to pay for the food in the communal kitchen?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who provided it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Red Cross-- the Red Cross. But that was only a few-- few days, a few weeks, you know? And after, when we get-- we go to the black market, and there she has the gold. So we get the food. And we can go back to normal. And I-- I cooking and cleaning. And then I find it in the basement-- I find a machine-- of-- with the--

speaker: interviewer
text: A sewing machine.

speaker: interviewee
text: --sewing machine. And I said to her, do you know what? I started to make some clothes to the children, to her children. And then I started to make her a-- a frock.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to her, you know what? I started to be sewing, and I will keep a housekeeper for you. And I will make a-- a living. Because-- And you-- you still lived with her, though?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yes. And I working with the-- to the Russian woman, you know, the baryshnyas. They have no taste and no-- don't know what to wear and how to wear it. And they bring me some nightgowns to make an evening frock out it. And I do it for them. And they bring me sugar, and ducks, and chickens, and things. And I was a rich girls, you know, because I have food. And it was everything-- everything for the food at that time to surviving. And I even get some jams. And I-- I make a parcel, and I go to the priest. I give it to them and bring it to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my cousin said, what you doing? I said, they was good to me. I have to. I-- the first thing, I take some food for them, what they-- what I earned with the Russians. How long did you continue doing that for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do it that about till May. And then I meet my mother girlfriend who was a-- a-- our lawyer's sister. And she live behind our house. And she has a empty flat.

speaker: interviewee
text: She said to me, you know, come here. Live with me in this-- not-- not together. I have a separate flat, you know? She said, make it nice, and you are my girlfriend daughter. You are not alone, and I am not alone.

speaker: interviewee
text: She lost her family. Only she was alone. She has a cousin with her, a-- a man. And then how I earned the money-- I-- not the money, I mean the food-- so I meet a old friend who was a painter. I said, look. I give you two kilo sugar. Paint my flat. So he does. And then I meet a man, and-- another man who was a furniture making. I said, look. I have my furnishing, just need to be re-polished and things. I give you a two chicken and-- [LAUGHS] and a two kilo flour. And he, said, fix it. So he was happy. So I fixed it up nicely, the flat too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get the furniture from your stepmother?

speaker: interviewee
text: But-- well, I get the-- what-- I have to fight for it, you know. He said, the Russians are take it. I said, where?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I asked my cousin husband. I said come with me, because my dining room they said they take it over there. So I take a carriage. And I was the horse. I pulled the carriage. And I went house by house to looking my furnishing. And I went in, and I find the cupboard-- a beautiful dining room. In somebody else's place?

speaker: interviewee
text: In somebody else's place, yes. And it was one door was missing. You know, it was-- it was a door who open up and was a office table. You know, that time it was-- that was a fashion. And I don't find the-- the door. I said to my cousin husband, I said, it must be here. And I looking everywhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said-- and I heard-- in the shower, I heard a noise. And I went in. And it was a naked Russian showering, and step on my door, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went in. I pull him [LAUGHS] up here. I take off the [LAUGHTER] door. And I lock-- close the door. And I have my furnishing. I have my doors. And he was so surprised. He [LAUGHS] was surprised, not me. And I runaway and take my carriage like I was the horse. And-- and I had my furnishing. And-- and I was hoping my family coming home. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: At this stage, did you find out that they weren't coming back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not yet, no. Only-- only in-- in August, the people coming home, you know, more and more people. And then they bring the news to the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any Jewish organization happening at this stage in Ujpest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but I tell you honestly. They was not so straight. And I don't like not straight people. They said to me, yes, we give you that, and that. But-- and I don't like the but.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were some of the buts?

speaker: interviewee
text: To be nice to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, the boys, the-- the mens over there, and I was young-- and to be nice, to be-- come out with me. And I said, no, I don't want it. I only want which is mine. And I will get it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you back to your name, or were you still with the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I back my name. Of course, I back my name. And then one day my-- that girlfriend of mine who's mother bring some food to my mother, they-- their house was bombed. And she has no home. So I tell-- told her, I said, come and live with me in the meantime, you know, when you can get your own feet. Because his parents live in the country. They went to the country, because that-- they have no home. And after the Russians, the-- you know, that part of the people was pushed away, you know, that the-- who were--

speaker: interviewer
text: The rich people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And then not only the rich people, but that-- that other-- the-- the-- who was serving the-- the German politics. And her father was there, even if they don't think the same way, but they work in the parliament, you know? And-- and they-- they push him away. So she need my help. So I help her.

speaker: interviewer
text: She came to live with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: She come with me, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did she have any remorse about what had happened to Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. You know, the people are funny. They-- they live their own life. And even how I always said, every non-Jews have a Jew friend, but they don't care so much-- not everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your friend didn't care?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they care, but they don't go out of their way. How shall I say? You know what I mean? Like after the war, I was thinking maybe they can tell me to go to the country with them, but they didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were you disappointed in them?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a way-- in a way, but then how my mother teach me and my [NON-ENGLISH] husband understand me, you can't change the people-- what they are. You have to take them how they are. You can't change them. So that's-- that's how I-- I take it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and one day, we went for a walk with my girlfriend that-- who live with me. And we meet my darling husband in the street. And he straight away come to me, and how I am, and how is my brother. And when somebody ask me my brother and my mother, I was already out of my way, you know? I was so happy to talk about them. And he said that because he was a scout, and my brother was a scout. And-- and he take us home. And he said, can I come to see you? I said, why not? You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he started to come. And I cook him a nice chicken soup. And you know, and he was alone. He lost his family.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my girlfriend said, that's the boy for you. I said, Cat, how can you say that? That's a-- we are not thinking of that way yet, you know, it's-- I was so, so upset-- the whole things. I was not over it yet, you know? And then the coming Rosh Hashanah-- and I light the candles from front of my family's picture. And my husband come in, and I was crying. And I said to him, I said, why I survive for what? I am alone here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, to make me happy. And that's how we get together, really. That's the story of my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We married in-- in September. And he said, he-- he want to marry me. And that he said, but not yet. I said, no. I not-- don't need a boyfriend. Sorry. We get married.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, when? I said, I take you to the priest, and I will tell you. And I take him over there to introduced him. To the priest?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the priest, of course. Had he--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes

speaker: interviewee
text: --was my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes? And he said, and when you want to get married? I said, in four weeks time. My husband look at me, [LAUGHS] and he said, and how? I said, I will fix it. And I ask, and I fix it. In four weeks time we was married.

speaker: interviewer
text: A big wedding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in-- in his place, in the priest's place.

speaker: interviewer
text: But was it a Jewish wedding?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you have, a civil wedding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: So who married you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The priest.

speaker: interviewer
text: The priest married you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah!

speaker: interviewer
text: So was it like a Catholic wedding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So I married three times. So that's why my marriage working. First time married in the town hall. Then I married in his place.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the church?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And then before we come out from Hungary, we go to the Rabbi, and we have a ceremony. So I have three weddings. And that's why my marriage was for 45 years so nice and good. And I never tell that anybody. I only telling you. Even my children don't know we-- I have three marriage. [LAUGHS] I never tell them.

speaker: interviewer
text: They're going to know now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [LAUGHTER] Maybe we leave it out, then.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHTER] Elizabeth, you stayed in Ujpest after you got married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: For how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have my little girl born there around-- she was one year old. We-- because they ask me. They wanted to give me some positions to the-- [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: In the Communist party.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And-- and I don't want it. No. It was not my way life. And my husband, my dear husband said, look, mommy, we have to go away, because you tell what you want to say. And that's no good for you. They put you in jail. So-- and then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your husband working at this stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: He has a-- a leather shop.

speaker: interviewer
text: Leather?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And he earned a very good money. We was very--

speaker: interviewer
text: His own shop?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, his own shop with his cousin. They was partners.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was his family in leather always? Was that the family business?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, he was in the perfume business. He selling before the war. But he-- he's-- he learned, because every Jewish boy have to have something--

speaker: interviewer
text: A trade.

speaker: interviewee
text: --a trade, so he learned a gloves making. He was a very good gloves maker. And then after the war, he-- he was working-- given the perfume. He loved those things that, you know, he was very particular. And after the war, he opened the shop, leather shop, with his cousin. And he make a very good money. And we live nicely and comfortable. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you decided to leave anyway?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We walk. We walk for two [NON-ENGLISH]. We go to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Two borders.

speaker: interviewee
text: --two borders to Czechoslovakia, and then Czechoslovakia to Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: With your baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my one-year-old baby. We pay the men who carry him-- carry her, but when she started to cry, they give it to me. So I have to carry her. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: How far did you walk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. It was a nightmare. And-- and even-- when we went to Czechoslovakia, then that was a shock of my life. The-- the men who we paid a good dollars in Hungary, he disappeared and let us down. And then we have to find ourselves. And we don't speak-- we only speak Hungarian. And we find somebody who said, for money, we take for my little girl, a dolly. And we put some dollars and gold in the-- in the-- in the dolly, inside. So every time when somebody asking, we opened the dolly and we give some money. And [LAUGHS] we-- we go further and further. And they put us in one room. And they put a cupboard in front of it. And they said in 24 hours you can be here, and then you have to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: So next night was Friday night. And I said to myself, I go to the synagogue. You don't, daddy. I go. So I went to the synagogue, and I looking around-- Where was this? In which town?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in Kosice.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, I was looking around in the synagogue, and I go to one of the men. And I said, look. We are coming from Hungary. I have a little girl and a husband here. We want to go through further. Could you help us?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, you know, you let somebody buy you a ticket to the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Railway.

speaker: interviewee
text: --railway. And you go to [NON-ENGLISH] And from [NON-ENGLISH], you can go further, to Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we'll finish in a minute.
